title: Why the return of your voice after years of silence is the real victory
author: Kalesh

The silence that wasn't silence at all 

 I remember the exact moment I realized my voice had gone missing. I was sitting in a coffee shop, and someone as...
htmlContent:
The silence that wasn't silence at all

I remember the exact moment I realized my voice had gone missing. I was sitting in a coffee shop, and someone asked me a simple question. What kind of tea did I want? And I couldn't answer. My throat closed up. My chest tightened. My mind went completely blank. I pointed at the menu. The barista nodded, confused. I walked away with a cup of something I didn't even want.

That wasn't the first time. It had been happening for years. Decades, honestly. Every time someone asked me what I needed, what I felt, what I wanted, my voice just... evaporated. I thought I was shy. I thought I was broken. I thought something was wrong with me.

Here's the thing. I wasn't shy. I wasn't broken. I was traumatized.

When you grow up in an environment where your voice is dangerous, your nervous system learns to shut it down. Bessel van der Kolk, the researcher behind The Body Keeps the Score, documented this extensively. He found that trauma survivors often lose access to the parts of the brain responsible for language and self-expression. The Broca's area, the part that turns experience into words, literally goes dark. You can't speak because your brain has decided that speaking is too risky.

Stay with me here. This isn't about being quiet. This is about survival. Your body made a decision a long time ago that your voice could get you hurt, abandoned, or worse. So it took your voice away. It locked it in a vault. And it threw away the key.

But here's what nobody tells you. The key was never really gone. It was just buried under years of fear, shame, and protective silence. And finding it again, one small piece at a time, is the real victory. Not the big dramatic breakthroughs. Not the cathartic screaming sessions. The small moments where you say "no" to someone. The times you ask for what you need. The seconds where you speak and don't immediately apologize for existing.

That's the return. That's everything.

What your silence was protecting

Let me be really direct with you. Your silence wasn't a weakness. It was a strategy. A very intelligent, very creative strategy that kept you alive in a situation where speaking could have cost you everything.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma freezes the body's natural responses. When you couldn't fight or flee, you froze. And freezing includes your voice. Your vocal cords lock up. Your diaphragm tightens. Your breath becomes shallow. You go quiet because quiet was safe.

Think about it. If you grew up with a parent who punished you for disagreeing, your voice became a liability. If you were told to shut up, stop crying, or stop making a scene, your voice became a threat. If someone you trusted used your words against you, your voice became a weapon aimed at your own heart.

So you learned. You learned to swallow your words. You learned to disappear into silence. You learned that being seen and heard was dangerous, so you made yourself invisible. And that worked. For a while. It got you through. It kept you safe.

But here's the problem. The strategy that saved you as a child is now strangling you as an adult. The silence that protected you is now the prison you live in. And breaking out of that prison, even just a little bit, feels terrifying. Because your nervous system still believes that speaking will get you killed.

Wild, right? Your body is still fighting a war that ended years ago. The enemy is gone. But the alarms are still blaring.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker, the author, writes extensively about the "freeze" response and how it s as silence, isolation, and disconnection from your own voice. Reading it felt like someone finally explained the secret language of my own body.

Your silence was protecting something precious. It was protecting your ability to survive. But now you get to choose. You get to decide if that protection is still needed. And most of the time, it's not. Most of the time, the silence is just a habit. A ghost. A story your body tells itself that no longer applies.

The voice returns in whispers, not shouts

I want to be honest with you about something. The return of your voice is not a dramatic Hollywood moment. It's not a single cathartic scream that changes everything. It's not a confrontation with your abuser where you finally say all the things you've held inside for decades.

It's much quieter than that.

It's the moment you tell your partner you don't want to go to that party. It's the time you ask your boss for a deadline extension. It's the sentence you say to a friend: "Actually, that hurt my feelings." It's the two words you whisper to yourself in the mirror: "I matter."

Those small returns are everything. Because each one is a message to your nervous system. Each one says: "See? I spoke. And I'm still here. I'm still safe. The world didn't end."

Janina Fisher, a leading expert in trauma treatment, talks about how the traumatized self needs to be gradually re-introduced to safety. You can't just jump into the deep end of self-expression. You start in the shallow waters. You practice saying your own name out loud. You practice telling someone what you want for dinner. You practice saying "no" to something small and meaningless.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did your chest tighten? Did your throat close? Did you feel a wave of resistance? That's the protective part of you, the one that kept you alive, screaming that this is dangerous. Thank it. It did its job. And then gently remind it that you're an adult now. You have choices. You have resources. You have a voice that deserves to be heard.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. He created Internal Family Systems therapy, which is all about understanding the different parts of you that hold these protective strategies. Your silence isn't a defect. It's a part of you that took on a really hard job. And that part deserves compassion, not punishment.

The voice returns in whispers. And those whispers, over time, become sentences. And those sentences become conversations. And those conversations become the life you were always meant to live. But it starts with a whisper. A single, trembling, terrifying whisper. And that whisper is victory.

Why the wellness industry gets this wrong

I need to say something that might make you uncomfortable. The wellness industry has done a massive disservice to people recovering their voice. They sell you the idea that you just need to "speak your truth" and "set boundaries" and "find your authentic voice." As if it's that simple. As if years of trauma can be undone by a mantra and a yoga class.

Look, I'm not saying those things are useless. I'm saying they're incomplete. They skip over the part where your nervous system is screaming at you that speaking will get you killed. They ignore the freeze response. They pretend that silence is just a choice, not a survival adaptation.

Toxic positivity tells you that your voice is already inside you, just waiting to come out. And that's partially true. But it ignores the fact that your voice was deliberately, systematically, and repeatedly silenced. It wasn't just hiding. It was traumatized. And trauma doesn't respond to affirmations. It responds to safety, patience, and repetition.

Deb Dana, a leading expert on Polyvagal Theory, talks about how we need to "befriend" our nervous system before we can ask it to do anything new. You can't just demand your voice back. You have to show your body, over and over again, that speaking is safe. You have to prove it. And that takes time. It takes failures. It takes days where you can't speak at all, and that's okay.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It walks you through exercises that help you reconnect with your body and your voice in small, manageable steps. No pressure. No demands. Just gentle, consistent practice.

And when you do start speaking, you need boundaries. Real ones. Not the Instagram-quote version of boundaries, but the messy, uncomfortable, real-life version where you tell someone "no" and they get upset and you don't take it back. A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) by Nedra Glover Tawwab. It's direct, no-nonsense, and it doesn't pretend boundaries are easy. Because they're not. But they're necessary.

The wellness industry wants you to believe that recovery is linear and beautiful. It's not. It's messy. It's two steps forward and three steps back. It's days where you can't speak and days where you can't stop speaking. It's progress that looks like regression. And that's not failure. That's the actual process.

The real victory is in the ordinary

I want to leave you with something that took me years to understand. The return of your voice is not measured by the big moments. It's measured by the ordinary ones.

It's ordering your own coffee. It's telling a friend you're not okay. It's asking for help. It's saying "I love you" first. It's saying "I'm angry" without apologizing. It's saying "I need space" without explaining why. It's saying "I don't know" and being okay with not knowing.

These are the victories that matter. They don't make it onto social media. They don't get applause. But they change everything. Because each one is a crack in the armor of silence. Each one is a message to your body that the world is different now. That you are different now. That your voice is safe.

Sit with that for a moment. Your voice is safe. It might not feel that way yet. Your nervous system might still be screaming warnings. But the truth is, your voice is safe. And it's been waiting for you to come back to it.

The return of your voice after years of silence is not a destination. It's a practice. It's something you choose, over and over again, even when it's hard, even when it's scary, even when you want to disappear back into the quiet. You choose to speak. And that choice, made thousands of times, is the real victory.

You're already doing it. Right now. By reading this. By showing up. By being here. That's your voice, too. It's the voice that says "I want to understand." It's the voice that says "I'm ready to try." And that voice, however quiet it may be, is already winning.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link)
  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link)
